votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by filtering digits, sorting them in descending order, mapping them to words, and comparing the result to the given answer.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by filtering digits between 1 and 9, sorting them in descending order, mapping them to their word representations, and comparing the result to the given answer.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by filtering digits, sorting them in descending order, mapping them to their word representations, and comparing the result to the given answer.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: is_digit checks 1 <= x <= 9, to_word maps digits 1-9 to their word equivalents, and by_length_spec filters digits, sorts them in descending order (matching Z.ge/Sorted), maps to words, and checks equality with ans. The Coq spec uses existential quantification over permutations that are sorted descending, which uniquely determines the sorted descending list, matching Python's sorted(reverse=True).

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: is_digit checks 1 <= x <= 9, to_word maps digits 1-9 to their word equivalents (with 'Nine' as default for 9), and by_length_spec filters digits, sorts them in descending order (matching Z.ge/Sorted), maps to words, and checks equality with ans. The Coq spec uses existential quantification over permutations that are sorted descending, which for any concrete input has exactly one canonical sorted result that Python computes directly.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq spec: is_digit checks 1 <= x <= 9, to_word maps digits 1-9 to their word equivalents (with 'Nine' as default for 9), and by_length_spec filters digits, sorts them in descending order (matching Z.ge/Sorted), maps to words, and checks equality with ans. The Coq spec uses existential quantification over a permutation that is sorted descending, which for any concrete input has exactly one such permutation (the descending sort), matching what Python computes.

Both filter elements to digits 1..9, sort them in non-increasing order, and map to the same word labels; the Python picks the canonical sorted witness for the Coq existential, so they agree on all integer inputs.

Both filter inputs to digits 1..9, order them non-increasing, and map 1..9 to words. The Coq spec requires ans to be map(to_word) of some descending permutation of the digits; Python picks the canonical descending sort, which is the unique such list up to equal elements, so results coincide on all inputs.

Both filter values 1..9, sort them in nonincreasing order, and map to the same word strings; the Coq existential with Permutation and Sorted uniquely determines the same descending list, so the Python check matches the spec on all integer inputs.
